section.name: session-management
section.summary: tmux session lifecycle commands, named-session conventions, session templates for project layouts, nested tmux pattern, and agent control via send-keys/capture-pane.

list.item: tmux new-session -d -s <name> — create detached named session
tmux attach -t <name> — attach; creates session if it does not exist (with -d)
tmux has-session -t <name> — check existence (use in scripts before attach)
tmux kill-session -t <name> — destroy session and all its windows/panes
tmux ls — list all sessions with window counts and activity times
prefix + s — interactive session picker (fzf-powered with tmux-fzf plugin)
prefix + d — detach from current session (session stays alive)

rule.statement: Always use has-session before creating a named session; re-running a session-creation script should attach to the existing session, not fail or create duplicates.
rule.rationale: Session scripts are often called from aliases, shell startup, or agents. If the session already exists, recreating it destroys ongoing work.

rule.statement: Use different prefix keys for local and remote tmux to avoid double-prefix confusion: Ctrl+A locally, Ctrl+B remotely (or vice versa).
rule.rationale: When inside a local tmux session and SSH'd into a remote tmux, every prefix command requires sending the prefix twice (once for local tmux to ignore, once for the remote). Different prefixes eliminate this ambiguity.

example.code:
# Local ~/.tmux.conf: Ctrl+A set -g prefix C-a
# Remote ~/.tmux.conf (on server): keep default Ctrl+B # OR: bind a distinct key like Ctrl+B on the remote
# To send a prefix to the inner (remote) tmux: # Press your local prefix (Ctrl+A), then Ctrl+A again to pass through bind C-a send-prefix

section.name: agent-control
section.summary: Controlling background processes from scripts or agents via tmux send-keys and capture-pane.

example.code:
# Send a command to a specific pane (non-interactively from a script) tmux send-keys -t "nero:logs.0" "journalctl --user -f -u nero-core" Enter
# Capture current pane content for programmatic inspection tmux capture-pane -t "nero:monitor.0" -p
# Check if a specific string is visible in a pane tmux capture-pane -t "nero:monitor.0" -p | grep -q "All OK"
# Send a key sequence to a pane (e.g., Ctrl+C to stop a process) tmux send-keys -t "nero:run.0" C-c tmux send-keys -t "nero:run.0" "q" Enter

antipattern.description: Sending commands to tmux panes as a substitute for proper systemd service management.
antipattern.reason: tmux panes are ephemeral user-space processes. Commands sent via send-keys do not survive server reboots. Use systemd user services for persistent processes and tmux only for interactive monitoring.
